item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting premise, but tone and vocabulary is pushing it in the downvote direction for me.

an offending section
– hide block

"The immune system always ignores the fungus, and the reason for this is as of yet unknown"

The above sounds off to me, and could be rewritten. I suggest something like:

a suggested change
– hide block

"SCP-4238 particles have evaded mammalian immune response in all known cases of infection. The mechanism responsible for this phenomenon has not been conclusively determined."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a few curiosities.

Why only mammals? Can the fungus only survive inside warmer bodies? Does cold inhibit or kill it? If that is so, it should also be able to infect birds (warm body temperature), as birds love berries, can fly and act as an easy dispersal method.

As for the vague immune evasion, could you perhaps look into a way for that to happen? I found a table describing various methods actual pathogens use: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867406001322#tbl1

Also, why does it have to scrape its skin off? Why not have the epidermis sprout fruiting bodies that expel spores, perhaps when the infected host thrashes around once it arrives at target, or some other action. You already have the SCP infecting the dermis, so this seems like a logical next step, especially since we're talking a fungus.

Also, why does it have to be in the spleen? Perhaps the early stage can live in the blood, then moves to the dermis for the next stage, and the final part of the life cycle modifies the epidermis?

Just some thoughts from a biologist.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: This isn't actually true, you can definitely link successful articles to Series I stuff. I've done it at least five times, and the last Series Contest was clearly won by an article that did so with enthusiasm. You generally need to be saying something interesting about that article with your link, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't get the point of the first part of the interview. There's no reason for the Agent to confuse them with this whole "Subject" thing, and it just comes across as incompetence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I must compliment you. This is one of the few articles that's actually putting up a fight against being deleted.

item.title: Touch-up the interview log
item.content:encoded:
Considering this was your first SCP it's not bad at all. Very formal tone throughout, although the part where you brush up on the immune system was a bit odd on how it was described.

"The immune system always ignores the fungus, and the reason for this is as of yet unknown"

I feel like the foundation wouldn't just leave it at that, if anything it would say something along the lines of:

"The reason for this is currently under review" or "The reason for this is due to the development of acute Immunodeficiency within the subject"

In regards to the interview log the subject's speech shifts in tone many times.

"I was always carrier. There was never a time I didn't have it in me" could be shifted to "I always carrier, always inside me"

Just odd shifts in vocab, like saying intelligence and then switching to smartest. But overall, it was a nice read. +1 because I liked it and I don't think it should be on the lowest rated category.

item.title: Pretty good
item.content:encoded:
Some of the phrasing was weird (The median iq went down by 50%? Odd measuremant to use), but it was pretty interesting nonthless. A bit sad that I found this in the "Lowest rated" catagory, but it's better than not finding it at all!

Cheers, Wakawaaa.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short and sweet SCP, I enjoyed it.

I'd advise going over the interview log again, though. Making the subject appear actually stupid is gonna be way harder than it's worth, I think. "I no remember" speech cringed me out a little bit, as well as "Hello, Subject." I'd advise replacing Subject with [DATA EXPUNGED] to imply that the interviewer used the person's previous identity. as well as "formerly known as [REDACTED]" to "formerly [DATA EXPUNGED]". This implies that the identity was wiped off the record entirely. Or, it might even be a better idea to make up a name like Karliah Jones and replace that same number of characters with full block icons. Up to you.

Hell, I'd remove the whole IQ lowering thing overall and make the person behave as if they're normal but memory-wiped and suddenly outside the control of their actions. I think that'd be scarier, anyways.

Also, as another commenter said, removing the spleen, in particular, seems a bit unnecessary. Writing "yo, if you find a -2, chuck his ass in the incinerator" in a clinical tone is a perfectly acceptable containment measure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some good bones, but feels like it still needs a few rounds of polishing. What stands out to me is the inconsistent Hulk Speech from "Subject" and the emphasis on IQ. IQ scores and the ability to form a coherent sentence are two different things, and it's especially weird to see it switch from "I no remember" to "I know that my blood grows the fungus."

Do you have a definite answer for how much the Subject understands about their condition? How much awareness they have left? Do they have a concept of lying? Of free will? Do they consider themselves an individual who serves the fungus, the fungus itself, something else? Is the fungus sapient separately from the host? You don't have to reveal all the answers in the article, and often less is more, but it'll help you write if you know the answers.

item.title: Re: Hey there.
item.content:encoded: For reference, the images used in this article come from here and here, both of which are public domain.

item.title: JS Site Crit Team Post
item.content:encoded:
While this is a good premise and there's horror here to be found, this was a -1 from me. As it stands, there are some issues with the tone and this article just comes out and hits you in the face with a lot of its details, like the ripping off the skin bit. In addition, there are several spots throughout where the author has chosen to bring in "will never" and other such strong and generally non-clinical language.

Why is the spleen being removed? I see later in the article that this appears to be the seat of the spore production so that makes sense, but if someone has a fungal infection, removing the spleen is likely a death sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is actually a real thing. Look up Zombie Ants

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 From me. I thought it was going to be typical zombies but this execution stood out to me. Im going to be inspecting blue berries closer now.

item.title: Hey there.
item.content:encoded: This is my first SCP i wrote. I hope you enjoy!

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: You're welcome. Thanks for writing a really interesting SCP!

For future reference please do not crosslink Series 1 SCPs in your own SCP. It annoys a lot of writers as we've already scene those SCPs dozens of times and it can feal like your trying to link to something popular to make your thing look better. Please only crosslink to later Series stuff unless really neccesary.

Having said that though I did enjoy this SCP. It's a great blend of creepy and weird and I like how you didn't make the infected people be aggressive. Nice work!
